source_metadata.title_raw: John A. Abaci - A-19-230 - December 11, 2019 - Sonoma

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
John A. Abaci 
Assistant City Attorney 
Walter & Pistole 
670 W. Napa Street, Suite F 
Sonoma, CA 95476 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-230 
Dear Mr. Abaci: 
December 11, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Amy Harrington, the Mayor of the City of Sonoma, take part in a governmental 
ordinance decision that would permit ( or prohibit) wireless network facilities to be installed in 
residential districts, given that such a decision would affect Verizon's ability to pursue a (currently 
suspended) application 'to install such network facilities across the street from her residence? 
CONCLUSION 
No, Mayor Harrington may not take part in the ordinance decision, given that the 
installation of facilities directly across the street would have a foreseeable, presumably material 
effect and no clear and convincing evidence indicates the decision would have no measurable effect 
on her real property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-230 
Page No. 2 
Amy Harrington, the Mayor of the City of Sonoma ("City"), owns a single-family residence 
(her primary residence) located in a district zoned as "residential" under the Sonoma Municipal 
Code. Some time ago, ten applications were ~ade by Verizon Wireless ("Verizon") for use permits 
to install wireless telecommunications facilities on utility poles at ten sites located within the City's ' 
rights-of-way. Ultimately, Verizon agreed to move forward with three of the sites. The other seven 
sites (including a site that is directly across from the Mayor's residence) were suspended without 
action until final determinations were made as to the applications for the three sites Verizon chose 
to move forward with. Under the terms of the agreement, upon a final determination being made on 
the three proposed wireless facilities ," Verizon will have 30 days to re-initiate the seven suspended 
applications. According to the agreement, any applications that are not re-initiated within that time 
will be deemed to have been withdrawn by the applicant. Those applications that are re-initiated 
will be subject to the new ordinance that the Council will be considering for introduction on 
December 16, 2019 ("new ordinance"). 
The suspended application for the site across from the Mayor's residence consists of a 
wireless telecommunications small cell antenna and related equipment to be placed on a utility pole 
with a power cabinet installed on the ground next fo the pole. The pole location is directly across 
the street from the Mayor's residence and within 500 feet of it. In a follow-up email, you provided a 
copy ofVerizon's application (submitted to the City of Sonoma Planning Department), which 
includes photo simulations of the appearance of the small cell facilities to be placed on and to the 
side of the existing utility pole. The application indicates that the utility pole would need to be 50 
feet tall-seven feet taller than the current height of the pole. However, the City's visual review of 
the pole indicates that it may have already been heightened to approximately 50 feet since the time 
of the application. A four-foot cannister antenna would also be added to the top of the pole. A street 
cabinet would be placed on a concrete foundation behind the sidewalk at a location that appear to be 
approximately four to eight feet east of the pole. The photos provided indicate there is an oak tree 
directly behind the utility pole, which partially obscures an otherwise clear view of the pole from 
the front yard of the Mayor's property. It does not appear that the tree would obscure the view of 
the canister antenna or the street cabinet, however. As stated above, the application is currently 
suspended and may or may not be re-initiated at Verizon's discretion. 
The new ordinance would replace an ordinance that was adopted under the urgency 
circumstances described within Government Code Section 36937(b). On November 14, 2019, the 
Planning Commission held a public hearing and gave comments and recommendations to the effect 
that the new ordinance should be modeled on the provisions of the urgency ordinance possibly with 
some modifications being made along the lines of more updated ordinances in order to retain the 
maximum level of local control possible. 
The new ordinance that will be· considered on December 16th is still in the process of being 
prepared. The urgency ordinance that is currently in effect prohibits the siting of wireless facilities 
within residential districts in the absence of an exception required to comply with federal or state 
law. The new ordinance is anticipated to either create a ranking of preferences for districts in which 
wireless facilities could be installed (with residential being the "least preferred") or continue the 
prohibition on siting within residential districts that currently exists. Either of these alternatives 
would affect the Application if it were in fact re-initiated by Verizon at some point in the future. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-230 
Page No. 3 
Under Section 87100 of the Act, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." Section 87103 further specifies, "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member of his or her immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. Among 
those economic interests is "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).) Mayor Harrington's 
ownership of a singie-family residence constitutes an economic interest in real property under the 
~-
. 
A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official 
or the official's agency, including any decision affecting real property as described Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).) Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a 
decision, Regulation 18701 (b) provides the applicable standard for determining whether the 
financial effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable. Because Mayor Harrington's real property 
is not expressly involved in the decision before the City Council, Regulation 18701 (b) is applicable 
in this instance. Regulation 18701(b) provides, "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be 
considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial 
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
As pertinent to the facts provided, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which ans official has a financial interest, other 
than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision involves property located 
500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence 
that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property. (Regulation 
18702.2(a)(7).) 
Currently, a Sonoma city ordinance prohibits the siting of wireless facilities within 
residential districts in the absence of an exception required to comply with federal or state law. The 
decision before the City Council could maintain the status quo, or alternatively could remove that 
prohibition, such that wireless facilities could be built within residential districts-though 
residential districts would be the "least preferred" site for wireless facilities. It is reasonably 
foreseeable that such a decision could financially affect Mayor Harrington's residential real 
property, which is not only within 500 feet of a proposed site for a wireless network facility but is 
directly across the street from it. No clear and convincing evidence has been presented indicating 
that the decision would have no measurable impact on the official's property. Rather, if Verizon 
chose to re-initiate its application for the site in light of the new ordinance, the installation of a 
wireless network facility would affect the view from Mayor Harrington's residence. Accordingly, 


File No. A-19-230 
Page No. 4 
Mayor Harrington has a disqualifying financial interest under the Act and may not take part in the 
ordinance decision. 
Lastly, Regulation 18703 provides that "[a] governmental decision's financial effect on a 
public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected· and the effect on his or her 
financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment." (Regulation 
18703(a).) In the present context, significant segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of all 
real property or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b ). ) A 
unique effect on a public official's financial interest includes a disproportionate effect on an 
official's real property resulting from the proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
(Regulation 18703( c )(2).) Here, although the ordinance would theoretically affect all residential real 
property in the jurisdiction, it is foreseeable that one site Verizon has already made initial plans for 
is situated directly across the street from Mayor Harrington's property, and would impact the view 
from her property. Given the proximity of her real property to a potential wireless network facilities 
site, the decision at issue would have a unique effect on Mayor Harrington and the "public 
generally" rule is inapplicable. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

effect and no clear and convincing evidence indicates the decision would have no measurable effect 
on her real property.

QUESTION: May Amy Harrington, the Mayor of the City of Sonoma, take part in a governmental 
ordinance decision that would permit ( or prohibit) wireless network facilities to be installed in 
residential districts, given that such a decision would affect Verizon's ability to pursue a (currently 
suspended) application 'to install such network facilities across the street from her residence?

CONCLUSION: No, Mayor Harrington may not take part in the ordinance decision, given that the 
installation of facilities directly across the street would have a foreseeable, presumably material 
effect and no clear and convincing evidence indicates the decision would have no measurable effect 
on her real property.
